concepts.name: Probability of an Event
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number between 0 and 1 measuring how likely something is — the fraction of equally likely worlds where it happens.
concepts.formal_gloss: For an event A in a finite equiprobable sample space, P(A) = |A|/|Ω|; in general, a normalized additive measure of likelihood assigned to events.
sources: Blitzstein & Hwang, Introduction to Probability ch. 1

concepts.name: Descriptive Statistics
concepts.intuition_gloss: Boiling a pile of numbers down to a few honest headlines — a typical value, a spread, and a picture of the data's shape.
concepts.formal_gloss: Numerical and graphical summaries of a dataset — measures of center (mean, median), dispersion (range, standard deviation, quantiles), and shape (histograms, skewness).

concepts.name: Random Sampling
concepts.intuition_gloss: Tasting a well-stirred spoonful to judge the whole pot — and understanding when the spoonful can mislead you.
concepts.formal_gloss: Selecting observations from a population so each unit has a known probability of inclusion; i.i.d. samples model observations as independent draws from a common distribution.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Study design and sampling

concepts.name: Conditional Probability
concepts.intuition_gloss: How your odds shift once you learn something — probability recomputed inside the smaller world where the clue is true.
concepts.formal_gloss: P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B) for P(B) > 0: the probability of A within the restricted sample space where B has occurred.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

sources: Blitzstein & Hwang, Introduction to Probability ch. 2

concepts.name: Independence
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two events are independent when learning one tells you nothing about the other — the coin has no memory.
concepts.formal_gloss: Events A and B are independent iff P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B); random variables are independent iff their joint distribution factors into the product of marginals.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

sources: Feller, An Introduction to Probability Theory vol. 1 ch. V

concepts.name: Bayes' Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: The rule for updating a belief when evidence arrives — flip 'probability of evidence given cause' into 'probability of cause given evidence'.
concepts.formal_gloss: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B), decomposing a posterior probability into likelihood times prior over marginal evidence.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Probability Distribution
concepts.intuition_gloss: The full profile of a random quantity — a curve or bar chart showing where its values land and how often.
concepts.formal_gloss: The measure a random variable induces on the reals, described by a probability mass function (discrete), density function (continuous), or cumulative distribution function F(x) = P(X ≤ x).
sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics, probability distributions entry

concepts.name: Bernoulli Trials & Binomial Distribution
concepts.intuition_gloss: Repeated independent yes/no experiments, and the resulting counts of successes — the fruit-fly of probability.
concepts.formal_gloss: For n independent trials with success probability p, the number of successes X satisfies P(X = k) = C(n,k) p^k (1-p)^(n-k).

concepts.name: Variance & Standard Deviation
concepts.intuition_gloss: How widely a random quantity scatters around its average — the typical size of its surprise.
concepts.formal_gloss: Var(X) = E[(X - E[X])²] = E[X²] - (E[X])²; the standard deviation is its square root, in the units of X.

resources.title: Khan Academy — Variance and standard deviation

concepts.name: Normal (Gaussian) Distribution
concepts.intuition_gloss: The bell-shaped curve that shows up whenever many small independent effects add together — nature's default histogram.
concepts.formal_gloss: The continuous distribution with density f(x) = (1/(σ√(2π))) exp(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)), closed under linear combinations and the unique fixed point of the central limit theorem.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — But what is the Central Limit Theorem?

concepts.name: Poisson Distribution & Process
concepts.intuition_gloss: The mathematics of random arrivals — raindrops, emails, radioactive decays — when events strike independently at a steady average rate.
concepts.formal_gloss: The Poisson(λ) distribution P(X = k) = e^(-λ)λ^k/k! arises as the limit of binomials with np → λ; the Poisson process is a counting process with independent, stationary increments of this law.

resources.title: Random (Virtual Laboratories in Probability) — Poisson process

concepts.name: Covariance & Correlation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A single number capturing whether two quantities tend to move together, apart, or ignore each other.
concepts.formal_gloss: Cov(X,Y) = E[(X-E[X])(Y-E[Y])]; correlation ρ = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y) ∈ [-1,1] measures the strength of linear association.

concepts.name: Law of Large Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: Flip a fair coin long enough and the fraction of heads is squeezed toward one half — averages of many trials settle onto the true mean.
concepts.formal_gloss: For i.i.d. random variables with finite mean μ, the sample average X̄_n converges to μ (in probability — weak law; almost surely — strong law) as n → ∞.

resources.title: Seeing Theory — Law of Large Numbers (interactive)

concepts.name: Central Limit Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Add up enough independent random nudges — whatever their individual shape — and the total's distribution morphs into the bell curve.
concepts.formal_gloss: For i.i.d. X_i with mean μ and finite variance σ², (X̄_n - μ)√n/σ converges in distribution to the standard normal as n → ∞.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — But what is the Central Limit Theorem?

concepts.name: Sampling Distribution
concepts.intuition_gloss: Imagine redoing your whole survey a thousand times — the spread of the answers you'd get is itself a distribution, and it's what error bars measure.
concepts.formal_gloss: The probability distribution of a statistic (e.g. the sample mean) viewed as a random variable across repeated samples; its standard deviation is the standard error.

concepts.name: Point Estimation & Estimators
concepts.intuition_gloss: Guessing a hidden number from noisy data with a formula — and judging the formula by how it behaves over many imagined repetitions.
concepts.formal_gloss: An estimator is a statistic θ̂(X₁..Xₙ) targeting a parameter θ, evaluated by bias E[θ̂] - θ, variance, mean squared error, and consistency as n → ∞.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Pick the parameter value under which the data you actually saw would have been least surprising.
concepts.formal_gloss: θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ L(θ; x) where L is the joint density of the data as a function of θ; under regularity conditions MLEs are consistent, asymptotically normal, and efficient.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Confidence Interval
concepts.intuition_gloss: An honest range around your estimate, built so that the recipe traps the true value a stated fraction of the time.
concepts.formal_gloss: A data-dependent interval [L(X), U(X)] with P(L ≤ θ ≤ U) ≥ 1 - α under repeated sampling, for confidence level 1 - α.

concepts.name: Hypothesis Testing
concepts.intuition_gloss: Assume the boring explanation, compute how surprising your data would be under it, and reject the boring story only if the surprise is extreme.
concepts.formal_gloss: A decision rule for a null hypothesis H₀ versus alternative H₁ controlling Type I error at level α; the p-value is the probability, under H₀, of data at least as extreme as observed.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

concepts.name: Bayesian Inference
concepts.intuition_gloss: Treat the unknown itself as uncertain: start with a belief, let each datapoint reshape it via Bayes' rule, and read answers off the updated belief.
concepts.formal_gloss: Inference placing a prior distribution π(θ) on parameters and computing the posterior π(θ|x) ∝ L(θ; x)π(θ), from which estimates and credible intervals are derived.
sources: Gelman et al., Bayesian Data Analysis ch. 1-2

concepts.name: Regression & Least Squares
concepts.intuition_gloss: Draw the straight line through a cloud of points that misses them least, then use it to predict one quantity from another.
concepts.formal_gloss: Modeling E[Y|X] = β₀ + β₁X (or Xβ in matrix form) with coefficients chosen to minimize the sum of squared residuals; geometrically, an orthogonal projection onto the column space of X.

resources.title: James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani — An Introduction to Statistical Learning

concepts.name: Experimental Design & Causal Inference
concepts.intuition_gloss: Randomly splitting subjects into treated and untreated groups so that any systematic difference in outcomes can only be blamed on the treatment.
concepts.formal_gloss: The design of data collection — randomization, control, replication, blocking — that licenses causal rather than merely associational conclusions; correlation alone does not identify causation.
sources: Freedman, Pisani & Purves, Statistics part I

concepts.name: Bootstrap & Resampling
concepts.intuition_gloss: When you can't resurvey the world, resample your own data with replacement thousands of times and watch how the answer wobbles.
concepts.formal_gloss: Estimating the sampling distribution of a statistic by drawing repeated samples with replacement from the empirical distribution, yielding standard errors and confidence intervals without closed-form theory.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.05 — Introduction to Probability and Statistics

sources: Efron & Tibshirani, An Introduction to the Bootstrap

concepts.name: Monte Carlo Methods
concepts.intuition_gloss: Answer a hard deterministic question by playing dice with it — simulate randomness many times and average what you see.
concepts.formal_gloss: Numerical methods estimating quantities (integrals, expectations, probabilities) as sample averages of random simulations, with error O(1/√n) guaranteed by the law of large numbers and CLT.

resources.title: Random (Virtual Laboratories in Probability) — simulation apps

sources: Robert & Casella, Monte Carlo Statistical Methods

concepts.name: Stochastic Process
concepts.intuition_gloss: Randomness unfolding in time — a whole movie of random values rather than a single random snapshot.
concepts.formal_gloss: A collection of random variables {X_t} indexed by time (discrete or continuous) on a common probability space, characterized by its joint finite-dimensional distributions.

concepts.name: Markov Chain
concepts.intuition_gloss: A memoryless random walk between states: where you go next depends only on where you stand now, not how you got there.
concepts.formal_gloss: A stochastic process with P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n = i, past) = P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n = i), encoded by a transition matrix whose long-run behavior is governed by its stationary distribution.
sources: Ross, Introduction to Probability Models ch. 4

concepts.name: Brownian Motion
concepts.intuition_gloss: The jittery path of a pollen grain kicked by invisible molecules — pure continuous randomness, the limiting shape of ever-finer random walks.
concepts.formal_gloss: The continuous-time process with B₀ = 0, independent stationary Gaussian increments B_t - B_s ~ N(0, t-s), and continuous but nowhere-differentiable sample paths.

resources.title: Random (Virtual Laboratories in Probability) — Brownian motion

concepts.name: Martingale
concepts.intuition_gloss: A fair game over time: given everything that's happened so far, your expected fortune tomorrow equals your fortune today.
concepts.formal_gloss: A process {X_n} adapted to a filtration with E[|X_n|] < ∞ and E[X_{n+1} | F_n] = X_n; optional stopping and convergence theorems make it a central proof tool of modern probability.
sources: Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples ch. 4

concepts.name: Entropy & Information
concepts.intuition_gloss: A measure of surprise: how many yes/no questions, on average, it takes to pin down the outcome of a random source.
concepts.formal_gloss: H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x), the expected log-loss of a distribution; relative entropy (KL divergence) D(p‖q) measures the inefficiency of assuming q when the truth is p.
sources: Shannon, A Mathematical Theory of Communication (1948)
Cover & Thomas, Elements of Information Theory ch. 2
